title: Q&A  Article in live   Le Monde's climate expert answers your questions about the extreme heat that recently hit France
subhead: Ask Nabil Wakim about how the country has handled this historic event – and how it will deal with the next one. He will answer your questions from 5pm Paris time.

US Supreme Court rejects Trump bid to end birthright citizenship
subhead: The US president had signed an executive order last year on the first day of his second term in the White House decreeing that children born to parents in the United States illegally or on temporary visas would not automatically become US citizens.

title: After Ukraine strikes refineries, Putin admits to 'a certain shortage' of fuel
subhead: The anger of Russians searching for gas at stations is beginning to emerge on social media. The Russian leader has admitted for the first time the impact of the deep-strike operations carried out by Kyiv, while downplaying them.

title: Mbappé, Dembélé and Olise lead Deschamps' attacking revolution
subhead: With 10 goals scored, France ranked among the most efficient teams in the World Cup's group stage. The French manager will once again rely on his forwards against Sweden on Tuesday in the round of 32.

title: In the DRC's Ebola epicenter, a 'cascade of deaths' and fear of contamination
subhead: Le Monde traveled to Mongbwalu, a town in the eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo, where the Ebola virus outbreak began. As the death toll continues to rise, local health facilities and NGOs have become overwhelmed, hampered by a shortage of resources.
byline: Morgane Le Cam and  Philémon Barbier

title: 'Their mouths, eyes, hands, were taped and they were thrown into the hole': Russian soldiers confront Putin with reality of front lines
subhead: A Russian exile media outlet has analyzed nearly 7,000 letters from Russian soldiers and their families to the Kremlin thanks to a security flaw.

title: 'The King and the Mockingbird': The animated masterpiece that inspired Studio Ghibli
subhead: His film took more than three decades to reach the screen. In 1946, Paul Grimault, then 41, began work on 'Le Roi et l'Oiseau' ('The King and the Mockingbird') with Jacques Prévert writing the screenplay. He finally saw it screened in 1979, at 74. The hand-drawn animation became a cult classic, inspiring generations of viewers and filmmakers.
